Post_Text: The claim that can end a charter school is usually protected by the smallest number on the policy. Your general liability says a million dollars. The abuse coverage hiding underneath it is often a sliver of that. Here is how to find your real number: [LINK]
The claim that can end a charter school is usually protected by the smallest number on the policy. Most leaders have never been shown that number. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Abuse and molestation coverage gets handled four ways on a school policy. A tiny sublimit buried in general liability. An outright exclusion. A monoline bolt-on stapled on from another carrier. Or adequate coverage built into a real school program. Most schools have one of the first two and have never been told which: [LINK]

A "$1 million" general liability limit can hide an abuse cap that is a fraction of that. Against what these claims actually cost, a fraction is the difference between an insured event and a closed school. Read your own sublimit before anyone else does: [LINK]

Your abuse and molestation coverage is usually the smallest number on the policy and the largest claim a school can face. Here is how to read it before you ever need it. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: Abuse claims often surface years, sometimes decades, after the events, because of when survivors are able to come forward. That makes the trigger and the date on your policy decide whether a past period is covered at all. This is not a technicality for a school: [LINK]

Even an allegation found to have no merit must still be investigated and defended. If your defense costs are paid inside the limit, every legal dollar quietly drains the coverage. Find out whether yours sit inside or outside: [LINK]

If the only way your broker can insure your school's abuse exposure is a separate abuse-only policy from another carrier, read that as a warning sign. A charter school with reasonable prevention controls should be able to get adequate coverage built into a real program. Here is what that seam is actually telling you: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance. If an abuse allegation surfaced next month, about a period years past, do you know what limit would respond, whether it sits inside your program or hides as a tiny sublimit, and whether anyone has ever weighed it against what these claims really cost? If you are not sure, read this: [LINK]
